params.text:
Secure handling in practice

Most of information governance comes down to sensible daily habits. Protect information wherever you are:

  • Use strong, unique passwords and never share your login. Don't write passwords on a sticky note by the screen.
  • Lock your screen when you step away and keep devices locked and encrypted.
  • Use secure email and check the recipient carefully before sending anything sensitive.
  • Don't discuss service users in public places such as corridors, lifts, buses or on social media.
  • Store paper records in locked cabinets and dispose of them by shredding or confidential waste — never an ordinary bin.

These small actions are your front line: they turn the principles into everyday protection for the people you support.

params.question: Which of these are good secure-handling habits in a care setting? Select all that apply.

answers.text: Locking your screen whenever you leave the desk

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Locking the screen stops others reading records while you're away.

answers.text: Shredding paper records or using confidential waste when disposing of them

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Sensitive paperwork must be destroyed securely, never put in a normal bin.

answers.text: Sharing your login with a colleague to save time

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Logins must never be shared — they identify who accessed what, and sharing breaks accountability.

answers.text: Checking the recipient carefully before emailing sensitive information

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. A quick check prevents the very common breach of emailing details to the wrong person.

answers.text: Writing your password on a sticky note stuck to the monitor

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. That makes it easy for anyone to gain access — keep passwords private and unwritten.

params.text:
Key takeaways

Information governance protects the people you support and their trust in you. Remember:

  • Follow the seven principles and use information lawfully, fairly and only as needed.
  • Treat health and care details as special category data deserving extra care.
  • Respect people's rights, including the right to see their own records.
  • Keep information confidential and share on a need-to-know basis — but share promptly to keep people safe.
  • Report any suspected breach immediately and build secure habits into every shift.
